TextENGNew: Shishibone City — Okinomiya Police Station
Shishibone City was, as far as provincial towns went, a rather run-down place.
changeReason: Japanese 廃れた means 'declining/run-down'; old translation's 'behind the times' implies cultural lag rather than physical deterioration.

TextENGNew: Okinomiya, tucked even deeper into the mountains, was more remote still.
With little to distinguish one season from the next, time seemed to drift and stagnate here — a thoroughly dull town.
No tourist sights, no local products to speak of.
Without even a thriving regional industry to its name, it was simply an unremarkable place.
Yet from the monotonous and peaceful townscape, it was difficult to glimpse the radical resistance of the Onigafuchi Guardians...

Even the Okinomiya Police Station — said to stand on the front lines of the dam conflict — exuded a listless air that gave no hint of the fierce struggles I had heard about at the prefectural headquarters.
"Good morning. Are you here to pay a fine?"
"No, I have an appointment with Hondaya-san from the Public Safety Division. Please tell him Akasaka is here."
"Ah... m-my apologies. Please wait a moment..."
The clerk — who had apparently assumed at a glance that I was there about a parking violation — fumbled with an unfamiliar extension number and relayed my arrival to the other party.
"Welcome, welcome! You must be Akasaka-san — what a long way to come! I'm terribly sorry we have to use this room; I did try to reserve a reception room, but..."
"A councillor suddenly showed up and chased me out! Wahahahaha!"
changeReason: Japanese お越しになった means 'came/arrived'; old translation's 'suddenly woke up' is a mistranslation - the councillor showed up, not woke up.

TextENGNew: It was a stretch to call this a conference room — it was a small, cluttered space that resisted the label.
The cramped area was lined with changing lockers, giving it the air of a smoking corner crammed into a locker room rather than any sort of meeting room.
Laughing with such coarse gusto, this Hondaya — head of the local Public Safety Division — did not strike me as a particularly intellectual man.
But in exchange, I could see that he carried absolute confidence in his gut instincts and years of experience.
"I apologize for intruding during such a busy time. I am Akasaka, from the Metropolitan Police Department. It's a pleasure to meet you."
"Hondaya, Public Safety Division. Good to meet you, good to meet you. Sankai from the Prefectural Crime Division threatened me to cooperate generously, so. Wahahahaha!"
"Crime Division? You mean the Prefectural Anti-Gang Unit?"

"Around here, we treat the Onigafuchi Guardians as an extension of the local yakuza, you see. If you think they're just a grassroots residents' movement, you'd have to be a real amateur — wahahahaha!"
Even the prefectural office had said that the Onigafuchi Guardians maintained ties to the local organized crime.
If I recalled correctly, the gang's leader was the adopted son-in-law of one of the village's influential figures...
changeReason: Japanese 組長 means 'gang leader/boss'; old translation incorrectly renders this as 'lieutenant'. Also 婿養子 means 'adopted son-in-law' not just 'adopted son'.

TextENGNew: "That's backwards. Completely backwards. Think of it this way: the yakuza operations came first, and the residents' movement attached itself on top as a convenient justification. Backwards, backwards. Wahahahaha!"
Hondaya erupted in laughter.
This differed slightly from what I had been told at the prefectural office, but someone embedded at the local level would naturally know far more about these things.
As we talked, detectives would occasionally drift in and out to change clothes.
One of them noticed Hondaya's roaring laughter and made his way over to us.
"I was wondering what you were all talking about. Nfu fu fu fu fu!"
"Oh, Kura-chan! Perfect timing — come on in. This is Inspector Akasaka, all the way from the Metropolitan Police in Tokyo."
"An Inspector...! I-I'm still quite new. Akasaka — pleased to meet you..."
"Nothing like that fresh-faced earnestness. Did they just hire you this year? Nfu fu fu!"
Suspecting I was being patronized, I only managed a polite smile and chose not to answer.
I could see that this new arrival, like Hondaya, was the hardened type who trusted instinct and experience above all else. Truthfully... I wasn't fond of people like that.
"Let me introduce you, Akasaka-san. This is Ooishi from the Criminal Investigation Division. If it's the S File matter you're asking about, he's the one to speak to."
"...The S File?"
"S for Sonozaki. The S File is a designation for cases involving the Sonozaki family — a sort of code name, you might say."
Sonozaki?
Now that I thought about it, I remembered seeing the name Sonozaki appear several times while reading through the documents at headquarters.
"...If I recall correctly, a Sonozaki was an officer in the Onigafuchi Guardians. The treasurer, I believe."
Both of them stared wide-eyed as I rattled off the Sonozaki's post without hesitation.
Then, a moment later, they burst out laughing together.
"Akasaka-san, was it? Na ha ha ha! You're quite the diligent student! Could it be you've memorized every major figure in the Alliance? Na ha ha ha ha!"
"I wouldn't say memorized, exactly — but I did read through the executive roster of the Onigafuchi Guardians."
"Nfu fu! Then who are the president and vice-president?"

"The president is the current mayor of Hinamizawa, Kiichirou Kimiyoshi. The vice-president is the head priest of the local shrine, Furude. The treasurer is Oryou Sonozaki, the auditor is Makino. The liaison chief is Yoshirou Kimiyoshi, the PR director is Sadao Sonozaki..."
"Na ha ha...!! Akasaka-san, you're something else! Pay's lousy, but would you like to work here? You're exactly the type we're most in need of and least able to find."
"Nfu fu fu fu! ...By the way, Sadao Sonozaki is head of the Youth Division. So close! The PR director is actually Tadataka Sonozaki. Nfu fu fu fu!"
It felt less like a compliment, and I settled into a faint sense of displeasure.
But I knew better than to let it show — this man was, if anything, the most indispensable person to my mission, and so I swallowed the feeling.
changeReason: Old translation says Akasaka 'was the weakest link in this investigation'; Japanese actually says Ooishi was 'the most indispensable person' to Akasaka's work - opposite characterization.

TextENGNew: I hadn't meant to let it show on my face, yet the seasoned detectives seemed to see right through me.
"Na ha ha ha! I didn't mean any offense. ...To make it up to you, what do you say I show you around the village?"
For someone with no feel for the area like me, that would be invaluable. I couldn't have asked for more.
"In the end, the Onigafuchi Guardians is nothing more than another name for Hinamizawa itself. ...Getting to know the village is the fastest way forward for your work. ...What do you say?"
At its core, a residents' movement was a matter of local power.
The leader of the movement was the leader of that power. The character of the region became the character of the movement itself.
As Ooishi had said, to truly understand the Onigafuchi Guardians — this anti-dam regional force — knowing the village was the quickest path.
"...Please, I would be grateful!"
I nodded back without hesitation, and Ooishi smiled with satisfaction and rose to his feet.
If I took to heart what the prefectural office and Hondaya had told me... if my identity were ever exposed, my personal safety could be at risk.
My adversaries were an extremist group with no reservations about using threats or violence against individuals.
If my personal information were leaked, it was not hard to imagine that the danger would extend well beyond working hours.
That thought brought with it a flicker of tension.
"These people are quick to remember faces. If they see you with me, it'll likely cause all kinds of problems for your work. It's a bit cliché, but put these on."
Ooishi had prepared an ostentatiously suspicious disguise: a baseball cap, sunglasses, and a face mask.
It would be unbearably hot, but Ooishi's warning was most likely right on the mark. Thinking so, I put it on with a measure of gratitude.
My appearance was so conspicuous that Ooishi could only offer a wry smile.
"...Now where did I leave off? ...Let's see..."
"Up to the part about the three old families that control the village."
The Onigafuchi Guardians was, in essence, the village of Hinamizawa itself.
It followed, then, that the Alliance's executives were directly the village's leaders — as Hondaya had explained to me a short while ago.
If three old families ruled the village, then those same three families ruled the Onigafuchi Guardians.
"What did the documents at the prefectural office say? About the leadership of the Guardians, and so forth."
Our documents were, in principle, classified.
Answering Ooishi's question would be a breach of confidentiality.
...But, most likely...
I judged that this man knew the situation far better than any document at the prefectural police headquarters, and so I decided to answer.
"The president of the Alliance is the current village mayor, Kiichirou Kimiyoshi."
Ooishi heard that and let out a small, dismissive laugh.
For him to ask as he did, and then to laugh off an answer drawn directly from the documents — that meant the reality was different.
"...Then there is someone else behind the scenes, apart from Kimiyoshi. Someone unknown to the prefectural office — or rather, to general knowledge."
"Nfu fu... Old man Kimiyoshi is nothing but a figurehead. In the first place, the post of village mayor is entirely ceremonial here."
"In other words... there is some other entity that truly controls both the Alliance and the village? Would that be the three families you mentioned?"
"Well, I'll explain all that shortly. ...Nfu fu fu fu!"
Suddenly, the car lurched — a sharp jolt, then settled.
The paved road had given way to gravel. At the same moment, the scenery outside the window transformed entirely.
"The Hinamizawa Dam Project Must Be Unconditionally Withdrawn"
"Shame on the Spineless Governor! Down with Centralized Authority!"
"Do Not Let the Dam Swallow Hinamizawa's Nature!"
"Protect the Village from the Wicked Dam!"
"Grudges Will Be Repaid! The Curse of Oyashiro-sama!"
changeReason: Japanese 怨念！ means 'grudges/resentment!'; old translation converts this to 'Fear the Wrath' changing from a declaration of grudges to a warning.

TextENGNew: "Smash the Dam! Bureaucrats, Get Out!"
"The Construction Office Must Come to the Table!"
"The Project Manager Must Negotiate With Us!"
Signs and banners crowded the roadside in clusters...
The words, slashed in hasty brushstrokes, were intimidating by their very appearance.
Yes — the point where the pavement had ended was, in truth, a border.
At that moment, I was startled by the car's sudden, hard braking.
I looked ahead — and there, blocking the road, was a barricade.
Five or six people dressed like activists, their faces layered beneath helmets and masks, were blocking the road and shouting angrily at us to stop.
"...W-What is this?! A checkpoint?!"
"Hmph. ...These people never learn."
Ooishi snorted and rolled down the window, leaning out.
"Hey, hey, hey — you really can't be blocking the road out here like this."
As Ooishi glared at them with a menacing smile, the men visibly flinched.
"...Clear the road, if you please. We can't get through like this. Nfu fu fu fu!"
Of all people... Ooishi's car. That panicked thought seemed to shatter their composure entirely.
"...Ah, if we had known it was your car, Detective Ooishi, we would never have been so rude. ...Detective, what happened to your usual car? In for inspection?"
"Something like that. Loaner cars never quite feel right, do they — nfu fu fu fu!"
From that brief exchange, I gathered that they had memorized the license plate of Ooishi's usual vehicle.
In other words, had they recognized it as a police car, they would have hidden the barricade.
What they were doing was plainly obstruction of a public road. An outright crime.
"...Who's that suspicious-looking fellow? You there — could you show us your face?"
The man fixed me with a menacing stare. My mask and sunglasses made me look all the more suspicious.
...Would refusing to show my face draw unnecessary trouble? I glanced at Ooishi for guidance.
"Give him a pass, would you? He's still a shy rookie — nfu fu!"
"...We only wanted to confirm he wasn't anyone suspicious. ...Hinamizawa has been unsettled lately."
"Na ha ha ha! Is your mirror at home warped? That mask on your face looks far more suspicious, don't you think? Na ha ha ha ha ha!"
The men and Ooishi continued the exchange with forced smiles, speaking in an amiable enough tone...
Yet a particular smell — like gunpowder, like tension — never left my nose throughout.
"Has the number of trucks illegally dumping still not gone down? You must have a real time of it, morning and evening both."
"It really helps when the police understand and cooperate with us... hahahaha..."
While that conversation played out, the barricade was shifted aside, leaving just enough of a gap for a single car to squeeze through.
Ooishi gave a light tap of the horn, and the car eased forward.
In the rearview mirror, I could see the men's hostile gazes fixed on the back of the car as we drove away...
"What was that just now? Blocking a public road like that...!"
"Of course it's a crime. But getting worked up over it won't get you anywhere. Nfu fu fu fu!"
"What those people claim, you see, is that trucks have been illegally dumping waste in the village. Their story is that they set up the checkpoints to screen suspicious vehicles and stop it."
"Illegal dumping? Like unscrupulous contractors disposing of industrial waste?"
"Their line of argument is that since the police aren't doing anything about it, they have to protect themselves. ...But the industrial waste story is something they arranged themselves."
"They arranged it themselves?"

"They have a clever strategy, these people. They've been scattering industrial waste around the dam site themselves to obstruct construction. Then they blame it on corrupt contractors and use that as their justification for the checkpoints."
"And then they stop every construction vehicle that comes through and harass it as much as they can. Nfu fu fu fu!"
"...One of the Onigafuchi Guardians' resistance tactics, in other words."
"Since the car's plate has been logged at that checkpoint just now, I don't expect any more inspection theatrics ahead."
"...Those people know well enough that the last thing they want is to make me angry. Na ha ha ha ha!"

"See, once they figure out a car is connected to the construction, word is they don't just stop it at every checkpoint — they throw rocks and scatter iron spikes in the road. ...You'd do well to be careful that your identity doesn't get out."
"If they ever found out you're from Public Safety, who knows what would be in store for you. Nfu fu fu fu!"
Ooishi talked as though this were all great fun, but for me there was absolutely nothing amusing about it.
"...It almost feels like we've strayed into a civil war."
changeReason: Old translation adds 'in the Middle East' not present in Japanese; new translation removes this unwarranted geographical specificity.

TextENGNew: "Na ha ha ha ha ha! That's a splendid way of putting it. It's pretty much exactly what you just described."
Ooishi turned toward me with a slow, menacing grin.
"This is a war zone, you see."
The car slowed a little as it traveled a single lane road cutting through rice paddies.
"Do you see that house in the forest over there? The old-looking one."
Following where Ooishi was pointing, I could indeed make out an old gassho-style farmhouse.
"That belongs to the village mayor — old man Kimiyoshi. You could call it the Kimiyoshi main house. There are many branch families. They're one of the Three Families."
"...The Three Families. The old houses that control Hinamizawa Village?"
"That's right. And the second one is the Furude family. There aren't even any branch families left — only the head priest's household remains."
"...Right over that way is the shrine. He serves as its priest. By the way, the Guardians' office is located on the shrine grounds."
The office of the Onigafuchi Guardians. If these people were the enemy, that would be their stronghold.
"Can we go see it?"

"The shrine is the Furude family's private property. Even as police, we can't set foot there without a warrant. ...They have a Diet member in their corner with ties to the village. ...Being police has its strengths and its weaknesses."
"Now, as for the Three Families — that goes back quite a long time. Apparently, in the old days, those three families would hold councils and decide things for the village together."
"Which means... it's different now?"
"...You have a sharp nose, don't you. That's what I'd expect from someone well-educated. Nfu fu fu fu!"
The car braked to a halt with a squeal.
Several signs were visible ahead of where we had stopped.
"Private Property. No Entry for Unauthorized Persons."
"Warning: Venomous Snakes! Danger — Turn Back!"
"Intruders Will Be Required to Sign a Bond Acknowledging an Entry Fee of One Million Yen."
changeReason: Japanese specifies intruders must sign a bond/document (証文に捺印); old translation omits this detail of forced document signing.

TextENGNew: Several signs bearing such notices had been erected there.
Stretching between the road and the forest was a chain-link fence wrapped in barbed wire...
"...This is private property, so this is as far as we go. There are surveillance cameras set up beyond this point. Claiming we simply lost our way won't wash with these people."
"...They're beyond this point, aren't they?"
Neither the Kimiyoshi family nor the Furude family — the ones who truly controlled Hinamizawa and the Onigafuchi Guardians from the shadows... the last of the Three Families.
"Yes. ...Beyond here is one of the Three Families — the Sonozaki family. They are the ones who control Hinamizawa from the shadows."
In the executive roster among the documents, names bearing the Three Families' surnames had indeed been numerous. Sonozaki was no exception. But the name always at the top was Kimiyoshi.
If Kimiyoshi was the public head, then Sonozaki was the hidden one.
This double structure itself quietly revealed that this village had two faces — one in the light, one in the dark.
"The head of the Sonozaki family is the current matriarch, an old woman named Oryou. Be careful — she's a formidable figure."

"She's enough of a VIP that even the city mayor greets her with the deepest bow. ...Well, her family ties in Hinamizawa and the surrounding towns amount to a voting bloc of several thousand, so no politician can afford to show disrespect. Nfu fu fu fu!"
By all accounts, this Oryou Sonozaki wielded such influence in the region that she was known by titles like Sonozaki the Empress.
"In other words... that person is the de facto leader of the Onigafuchi Guardians?"
"That's right."
With those words, Ooishi pulled a crumpled cigarette box from his breast pocket.
The kidnapping of a minister's grandson. The suspected extortion.
Was this the work of the Onigafuchi Guardians, operating in the shadows in pursuit of the dam project's cancellation...?
"...You came all the way from Tokyo to investigate this, didn't you?"
"...Hm? Ah — yes! That's right. ...Is something the matter?"
"Ah, then... it must be connected to that business with the direct appeal to Minister Inukai, is it? So Public Safety has them flagged as a group of concern. Something like that? Na ha ha ha ha."
"Having you read it so quickly makes this easier."
Exhaling cigarette smoke, Ooishi laughed broadly, as if I'd caught him off guard... or so I thought. His laughter cut off abruptly.
"That's not all of it, is it?"
"The business about them being flagged because of the direct appeal. That's not all of it, is it?"
Ooishi knew full well that Tokyo headquarters would never dispatch an investigator over something as minor as a direct appeal.
If someone was dispatched, there had to be a larger, more pressing reason. He had already arrived at that conclusion.
I had always considered myself the type who doesn't wear his thoughts on his face. But before this man's piercing gaze, I wondered if everything inside me had been laid completely bare...
Ooishi fell silent and lit a fresh cigarette in the heavy pause, as if waiting for me to crack.
...Ah. So this was a seasoned detective's method for getting a confession.
But having recognized it, that was that.
Staying silent here would cost me nothing.
All I had to do was enjoy the peaceful view outside until Ooishi gave up.
"...Nfu fu fu fu! You really are terrible at hiding things. Being so earnest and open like that isn't such a bad thing."
I showed no sign of yielding, and after his third cigarette Ooishi smirked and ground it out.
changeReason: Japanese says Akasaka showed no sign of yielding; old translation incorrectly says he 'had yielded completely,' which is the opposite meaning.

TextENGNew: At first I had thought I'd won — but I had certainly given him the conviction that I was hiding something.
In that sense, I may well have ended up dancing to his tune after all.
Ooishi started the engine again and set the car moving.
"...If you'd just been candid with me, I thought I might have been able to help you more."
"...And how would you help, if I were candid?"
The cat was already out of the bag, so I decided to turn the question back on him.
"Well, it depends on the particulars — but I might be able to arrange a meeting with someone well-informed about the goings-on in and around Hinamizawa."
"An informant?"
"...Nfu fu fu fu! Ah, so that's what you call it?"
"...Why are you interested in my mission, Ooishi-san?"
"Same reason as you, isn't it? Isn't it our job to always be in the know?"
The opportunity to make contact with Ooishi's informant was enormously attractive.
We needed to recruit someone with local knowledge to collaborate with on intelligence gathering.
In the end, no method could surpass simply asking a person who knew people.
But we had always been taught that the ordinary police were no better than a sieve.
Careless and sloppy with classified matters, unlike us.
The kind who might let something slip in a drunken moment — we regarded them with that degree of contempt.
Among them, Ooishi seemed a particularly unruly type. And yet here I was, wrestling with whether to tell him about the kidnapping of the minister's grandson.
"...This informant — how familiar are they, exactly, with the Onigafuchi Guardians?"
"Na ha ha ha! Being from Public Safety, you'd understand, wouldn't you? That's hardly something I could tell you, even you."
...Never reveal your sources.
That was the cardinal rule for anyone handling classified information.
In that regard, at least, Ooishi did not appear to be one of those careless types.
...Should I trust him and ask for his cooperation — or not?
"You work for the national government, I work for the prefecture. I don't think our jobs differ all that much. ...I believe I can be of help."
But it was hard to believe that this cunning old man would offer his assistance purely out of the goodwill he professed.
What was the right move here...
"...I find it difficult to imagine that someone of your standing would help me free of charge."
"Na ha ha ha ha... Well, I'm not entirely without ulterior motives. You see, your organization keeps a budget set aside to pay informants, doesn't it?"
"The kind that doesn't require receipts. ...Just a little of that would cover the cost of arranging the contact."
"In other words, you're telling me to pay you in exchange for arranging the meeting with the informant? ...At least the terms are clear."
To state an ulterior motive so brazenly — that was something of an achievement in itself.

A deal with a clear payment attached was far more straightforward than charity offered out of good will. I sensed instinctively that he was a man skilled at precisely these kinds of backroom arrangements.
"Don't misunderstand me — it's not me who needs the money, it's the informant. For procedural reasons, the payment passes through me first. Nfu fu!"
"When would you like it?"
"Right now would be ideal. Though tomorrow is fine too if you don't have it on you."
I reached into a wallet separate from my personal one.

The amount was roughly calibrated to the quality of information received, ranked in tiers. In this case, since payment was upfront and I had no way to gauge what I would get, I could not offer too much...
While I was mulling it over, Ooishi suddenly stretched out his hand and snatched the contents of the wallet.
"At times like this, you don't want to be stingy. It's fine, it's fine — you won't regret it."
"...Understood. I won't be stingy."
"And? What is it you want to look into? The approach may differ somewhat depending on the content..."
"...Would you swear not to repeat this to anyone else, Ooishi-san?"
"There's no need to swear. I'm the same as you. Information obtained in the course of one's duty cannot be disclosed."
"Na ha ha ha ha! All right, I swear. Nothing heard here goes anywhere else."
"Besides, thanks to you, I can finally settle a tab that's been running a while. When money is involved, I absolutely keep my word. Nfu fu fu fu...!"
Even by my own standards, this felt like it was in poor taste.
But... for better or worse, the proposal intrigued me.
"Understood. I will consider your offer depending on the details. ...So then — what kind of information do you people have?"
"Nahahaha! Straight to the high-handed approach. I might have to reconsider this arrangement a bit~"
"...What do you mean?"
A flash of irritation, and I glanced at Ooishi. He was still smiling as he leaned forward with quiet menace and said,
"I don't know how things work in Tokyo, but... what gets things done out here in the field is instinct and personal connections. Titles and career credentials count for absolutely nothing."
"Do you really think you'll hear anything useful, going about things that way?"

"In other words — you're in the kind of position where it would be perfectly natural for you to bow your head and ask for help. Do you understand now? So a little more care in how you speak to me would be greatly appreciated."
The words were polite, yet the manner of delivery was provocative to the point of brazenness.
I felt a brief flash of anger... but on reflection, there was indeed very little I could do on unfamiliar ground. He was right.
"...I apologize. I seemed to get carried away and said something impertinent. I hope you will forgive me."
"Not at all — if anything, forgive me. But it's good that you take it to heart. You're still young; there's no shortage of chances to correct course. Nahahaha!"
Then Ooishi spoke about the going rate for the 'expenses' that would be required to use his informant.
A fair portion of that would probably end up in his own pocket, but that much was within acceptable limits, I supposed.
I reached into a wallet separate from my personal one.

The amount was roughly calibrated to the quality of information received, ranked in tiers. In this case, since payment was upfront and I had no way to gauge what I would get, I could not offer too much...
While I was mulling it over, Ooishi suddenly stretched out his hand and snatched the contents of the wallet.
"At times like this, you don't want to be stingy. It's fine, it's fine — you won't regret it."
"...Understood. I won't be stingy."
"And? What is it you want to look into? The approach may differ somewhat depending on the content..."
"...Would you swear not to repeat this to anyone else, Ooishi-san?"
"There's no need to swear. I'm the same as you. Information obtained in the course of one's duty cannot be disclosed."
"Na ha ha ha ha! All right, I swear. Nothing heard here goes anywhere else."
"Besides, thanks to you, I can finally settle a tab that's been running a while. When money is involved, I absolutely keep my word. Nfu fu fu fu...!"
I wavered until the very last moment over whether to trust him... but in the end, I gave in.

Ooishi wasn't the kind of man I could trust unconditionally, but there was no doubt he had access to intelligence that lay beyond the usual channels. To carry out my mission swiftly, he was someone I needed to work with.
I resolved myself.
"...I am investigating whether the Onigafuchi Guardians is involved in a certain incident."
"A certain incident?"
"There is a possibility that the Minister of Construction, Inukai, is being subjected to coercion by unknown parties."
"...That's a rather roundabout way of putting it — 'a possibility'."

"It appears there is a mole close to the minister. Because of that mole, the minister has been under close surveillance, and by all indications capitulated at the very outset of the coercion. He has made no report to the police, nor to us."
"...I see. So that's why you only say there's a 'possibility' of coercion. ...But doesn't that seem strange? If the minister reported this to no one, how did you come to know about it?"
Without surveillance of the minister's private life, it would never have come to light.
"...I don't believe that explanation needs to be provided to the informant, so I'll omit it."
Ooishi had apparently already guessed, and when I refused to explain, he stifled a quiet chuckle.
"I see. ...So, there appears to be a spy near the minister, which is why Public Safety took it on as a strictly classified investigation. ...That is certainly no simple matter."

"If this were made public, the minister's political career would be finished, and the likelihood of a broader political upheaval would be extremely high. If that is the perpetrators' objective, the situation could become very grave indeed."

"...Well, the government's nationwide development projects have apparently created a great many conflicts with various parties. It wouldn't take much of a scandal before the opposition parties started gaining ground significantly."
"We are currently investigating what demands are being made of the minister. But the method of coercion, at least, we have largely identified."
"...What is it?"

"The kidnapping of the minister's grandson. Those close to him are denying it, but based on when he was last seen, it is believed that more than seventy-two hours have already elapsed since the abduction."
"Three days, then. ...If this were an ordinary ransom case, it would have been resolved by now."
"The probability is extremely high that they are demanding some form of political action from the minister rather than money. ...That is something we cannot allow to stand."
"So then, the Onigafuchi Guardians was listed among the possible perpetrators."
"That is correct."
"...Kidnap the minister's grandson to demand the withdrawal of the dam project... Hmm..."
"What do you think, Ooishi-san? ...From your perspective, does that seem plausible?"
Ooishi was quiet for a time, letting out a low murmur as if turning it over in his mind.
When I had posed the same question to the section chief at the regional office, he had answered immediately: "It's plausible."
Yet Ooishi, who knew so much more, was hesitating...
And then, quietly...
He said something that was just slightly strange.
"...This was before the Meiji Era. This area wasn't called Hinamizawa Village back then — it was known as Onigafuchi Village."
"...Nahahaha, this is just something my grandmother passed on to me."
The subject had shifted so abruptly from the kidnapping of the minister's grandson that I found myself caught off guard.
"Onigafuchi? ...Ah, so that's where the name 'Onigafuchi Guardians' comes from."
"...Onigafuchi Village was said to be a village inhabited by demons that devoured humans. Even now, the villagers believe that demonic blood runs through their veins."
"De-...demons that devour humans?? Ooishi-san... what on earth are you talking about?"
"Information. It's information. You gave me quite a lot just now — perhaps more than I should have taken. Consider this a little something in return."
Ooishi smiled slightly, but he did not appear to be joking.
"...Please, go on."

"These demons, you see — ordinarily they were revered as something like mountain hermits and lived in seclusion. But being demons that ate people, they would apparently come down to the villages at the foot of the mountain at regular intervals to carry off their prey."
"...That act was called 'Onikakushi' — being demoned away."
Onikakushi...
It was the first time I had heard such a peculiar word.
But the unsettling content — demons abducting people to devour them — left a lingering trace of dread.

"...You know how people commonly say 'spirited away'? When a person suddenly vanishes one day, that's what they call it. ...Around here, when the same thing happens, they call it being 'demoned away'."
"...Does that old legend have some bearing on the current matter?"
"...Oh, not at all. There's no direct connection — it's just a bit of idle chatter. ...It's just..."
The car shook. Without noticing when, the gravel road had changed back to pavement.
"Just? Just what?"

"...I merely wanted to mention that in this land, there is a term called 'Onikakushi' — a keyword that carries connotations of disappearance and abduction. Whether it has any connection to the kidnapping of the minister's grandson, I cannot say."
"...Well, there's nothing deep to it. Only that such a legend exists here — that's all. Nfu fu fu fu fu...!"
Onikakushi — the act of demons descending from the mountain to seize their prey.
Then — to force the withdrawal of the dam project that threatened to submerge their village — had someone carried off the minister's grandson and performed an 'Onikakushi'?
...In that case, had the minister's grandson already been made an example of — devoured alive by demons?
"...Hahahahaha. That's rather an intriguing old tale."
"Venture into the demons' village without cause, and you will be swiftly captured and eaten alive. So do not go near Onigafuchi."
"...These days, young people don't believe in superstitions like that. But among the older people of Okinomiya, those born in the Meiji Era, there are still many who take it quite seriously."
Hinamizawa Village, feared by those who lived around it. I recalled a piece of information I had heard from the Prefectural Public Safety Division.
"After all, this is deep countryside. Superstitions find their footing here quite naturally."
"Na ha ha ha ha! Someone from Tokyo like you, Akasaka-san, probably can't even begin to imagine it."
...The man-eating demons, as punishment for the violation of their village, had abducted the minister's grandson — and devoured him alive.
That absurd thought floated through my mind.
Such an idiotic imagining — I was half-disgusted with myself.
Ooishi had simply been pulling my leg.
...Foolish. Truly, utterly foolish.
...Come to think of it, perhaps by that point I had already fallen under the spell of that uncanny village.
...Realizing that... would take a little more time.
